title: Lifetime Economy Runs at Half-Speed for Country's Young
full_text:
Generation gap While some blame mass migration, economists say a 20-year productivity collapse has dramatically slowed the nation's economy and hit young people hard, writes Luke Kinsella.
Australia, we have a problem. A triple whammy of bad economic news this week would have had Treasurer Jim Chalmers sweating bullets and hoping people were more focused on the State of Origin.
First, Deloitte Access Economics said on Tuesday that Australia was on the brink of the longest stretch of sub-2 per cent economic growth since the 1990s recession.
Later that day, the OECD warned real wages in Australia would probably fall again this year, singling us out as one of the few countries to have made little progress on that front. The next day, the International Monetary Fund downgraded Australia's economic growth forecast for 2026 from 2 per cent to 1.9 per cent.
These are all setbacks for the Albanese government trying to move on from the "cost-of-living crisis". But it just won't go away. Almost 40 per cent of us, according to the latest The Australian Financial Review/Redbridge Group/Accent Research poll in June, say the cost of living is our top-ranked issue. The next-highest voter concerns are immigration and housing, both far behind on 10 per cent.
It seems voters are right to feel the country isn't heading in the right direction. An AFR Weekend analysis shows the 2020s are on track to be the worst decade for growth in Australian living standards on record.
Consumer confidence is near a record low and economists are saying we could be approaching an "economic slowdown we have to have", which they say may be needed to bring the 3.6 per cent underlying inflation rate back to the Reserve Bank of Australia's target of 2.5 per cent.
It's hardly what Australians want to hear after enduring a pandemic-induced recession, inflation at a 36-year high and, this year, a global energy crisis. Now we're told by economists and RBA governor Michele Bullock that the solution to this stubborn inflation problem is that unemployment must rise and growth must slow. Great.
Economists generally agree that gross domestic product per capita - the total value of new goods and services produced in an economy each year divided by population - is a good measurement of living standards.
Challenger chief economist and 25-year veteran of the RBA Jonathan Kearns has sounded the alarm that Australia is already in the middle of the longest GDP per capita recession in the 65 years of data published by the Australian Bureau of Statistics.
"This is the longest run of declining GDP per capita we've ever had," he says. "GDP per capita has recorded only four quarters of positive growth since mid-2022, and remains below its peak from almost four years ago."
It's the warning on wages that will have workers worried and contribute to a sense that things just aren't working.
"Real wages have regained some of the lost ground in virtually all OECD countries - real wages are near the trough of the cost-of-living crisis only in New Zealand and Australia," the OECD says in its latest employment report.
Real wages measure how much you earn, adjusted for inflation. So if prices rise faster than how much you earn, your income may be higher, but you can buy less with it. In other words, your real wage is lower.
Chalmers has trumpeted the Albanese government's progress on restoring real wages - boasting until late last year that real wages had been positive for eight consecutive quarters.
That streak was broken in the December 2025 quarter, and now real wages appear to be back firmly in the red.
"The biggest drop in real wages since the pandemic was the 3.5 per cent drop between March 2021 and June 2022 under the Coalition's watch," Chalmers said in response to the OECD report this week.
For the union-aligned Labor Party, making sure workers get ahead is an important barometer of its success. Even so, it has not made much progress on restoring that Coalition-era drop.
Kearns says the news may be better for lower-income earners, given the Fair Work Commission's decision to lift award wages by 4.75 per cent. The estimates of real wages used by the RBA are averages and may hide higher wage increases for some workers.
"The minimum wage increase was above inflation - only by a little bit - so that should mean that the lowest-paid workers get some real wage increase, and they're the ones who will need it most," he says.
But Kearns says when real wages decline, it means Australians' average standard of living is going backwards.
One of the best measures of standard of living is GDP per capita, which is 67.1 per cent higher than in 1990. That means the average Australian is wealthier than they were a few decades ago. That's the good news. The bad news: the rate of improvement has slowed dramatically.
Humans have what scientists call "improvement default", which means we assume societies get better over time. One study from 2023 by academics at the Queen's University in Canada said it is taught to us through cultural stories of triumph in books and other media. We like a happy ending.
So far in the 2020s, GDP per person has grown 0.7 per cent every year on average. In the 1990s, it grew at three times that rate.
A common explanation for this decline is high or "mass" migration. The argument goes something like: migration increases population, which shrinks the slice of the economic pie going to each individual.
"No serious economists would give that any credibility," says Kearns.
"It doesn't explain why the presence of new working migrants has reduced the productivity of everyone who is already here. If you have strong migration, but you have productivity growth, the economy grows by more because everyone's being more productive."
In other words, migrants increase Australia's population, but they also increase Australia's economic output. It could be the case if migrants are less productive than the average Australian, but Kearns says there is no evidence of that. In fact, migrants who make the often-expensive trek to Australia can be more motivated to work hard in industries with skills shortages.
"The reason we've had weak GDP per capita growth is because we've had weak productivity growth," says Kearns.
Economists say that productivity growth, which means the economy can produce more goods and services with fewer inputs (such as labour and capital), is what drives economic growth in the long run. The most common measure of productivity is GDP divided by the total number of hours worked by the labour force.
Over the past 20 years, such growth has tanked. The average growth rate over the past five years was just 0.1 per cent. In the 2000s, it was about 1.5 per cent, and in the late 1990s, it was consistently above 2 per cent.
Kearns says this could be contributing to declining sentiment about the economy among younger generations.
According to the Lowy Institute, optimism about the economy has dropped more sharply among people aged between 18 and 29. In 2023, 55 per cent of young people were optimistic about Australia's economic performance. In 2026, it has fallen to 34 per cent.
"If you're an older generation, your wealth is increasing because of your assets, like shares and housing. Your ability to afford a standard of living is increasing even if your wages aren't," says Kearns.
"But for younger cohorts, they don't have that asset base and so their standard of living is dictated much more by the earnings."
Our politicians are talking a lot about "intergenerational equity" lately. Chalmers made it a key theme of his latest budget, which raised taxes on the asset wealth common among older generations by reining in the capital gains tax discount and limiting negative gearing.
It is widely thought that young people, locked out of the housing market, are struggling to achieve the same standard of living reached by their parents.
But there is more to it than that. According to the Productivity Commission, younger Australians are still earning more than previous generations of youth and are also better educated. We also stand to inherit more wealth from our parents than most generations, even if it is not spread equally.
It's the first rung of asset wealth, mostly property, that is more unreachable.
Still, GDP per person is higher compared with many decades ago, and even before the pandemic. But is it growing as much as it used to?
To test whether this is real or just a bunch of whingeing from today's youth, we can calculate the average growth in living standards over each generation's lifetime. So the average growth for someone born in 1975 uses 50 years of data, and for someone born in 2008 it is based on 18 years of data.
This shows younger people today have grown up in an economy up to half as good as someone born before the 1990s.
If you were born between the 1970s and the mid-1990s, GDP per person has grown at about 1.5 per cent every year of your life. Australia's 18-year-olds born about 2008, on the other hand, have grown up in an economy with average annual growth of just 0.8 per cent.
Australia's 26-year-olds were kids watching their parents navigate the global financial crisis in 2008, teenagers during the decade of slow economic growth that followed it, and young adults during the pandemic-era recession.
They are in the early stage of their careers in an economy languishing amid record-low productivity growth, a global energy crisis and geopolitical tension taking precedence over traditional economic principles such as free trade, fiscal discipline and free markets.
They're working at a time when artificial intelligence is likely to replace the skills once considered guaranteed to land you a job, such as computer programming and data analysis. Or maybe it won't - but that doesn't make career decisions any easier while we all figure it out.
Young Australians are due an era of higher growth to lift their average experience of the economy, but the reports from the OECD, IMF and Deloitte this week suggest they will have to keep waiting.
It's not just in Australia, either. The United States, Canada and the United Kingdom are all suffering from a similar problem: a productivity lull and geopolitics getting in the way of economics.
There is a simple equation in history that goes something like: angry and pessimistic young people plus distrust in mainstream institutions equals support for alternative and populist solutions to economic problems.
In the US, the Republican Party - once the party of neoliberal economics and free markets - has rallied around a protectionist president. New York City, the home of billionaire Wall Street bankers, has elected a socialist mayor in Zohran Mamdani, who is promising rent control and big tax increases for the wealthy.
In Australia, 20 per cent of people consider either the Greens or One Nation to be the best party to handle the economy. A minority, 47 per cent, nominate one of the two major parties.
If Chalmers really wanted to help young people, he would zero in on productivity. If Australia had maintained the rate of productivity growth between 1995 and 2023 at the 2.2 per cent average of the 1990s, workers today would be earning $25,000 more a year. P
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Luke Kinsella
heading: Banking and Economy
